questions.questionText: Simple lipids are best defined as:
options: Esters of fatty acids with various alcohols
Lipids containing phosphate and nitrogen base only
questions.explanation: Simple lipids are esters formed between fatty acids and alcohols. Examples include fats, oils, and waxes.

questions.questionText: Triacylglycerols are esters of fatty acids with:
questions.explanation: Triacylglycerols, also called triglycerides, are formed when three fatty acids are esterified to glycerol.

questions.questionText: A fat in the liquid state is called:
questions.explanation: Fats and oils are both triacylglycerols. The difference is mainly physical state: fats are solid, while oils are liquid.

questions.questionText: Waxes are esters of fatty acids with:
questions.explanation: Waxes are simple lipids made from fatty acids esterified with high molecular weight alcohols. Examples include cholesterol esters and vitamin A/D esters.

questions.questionText: Compound lipids differ from simple lipids because they contain:
options: Additional groups besides fatty acid and alcohol
questions.explanation: Compound lipids contain fatty acids and alcohol, but also additional groups such as phosphate, nitrogenous bases, carbohydrate, or protein components.

questions.questionText: Phospholipids contain fatty acids, alcohol, and:
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are compound lipids that contain phosphoric acid in addition to fatty acids and an alcohol.

questions.questionText: Glycolipids contain fatty acids with:
questions.explanation: Glycolipids contain fatty acids and carbohydrate. They also contain nitrogen but no phosphoric acid.

questions.questionText: Derived lipids include:
options: Fatty acids, glycerol, steroids, and ketone bodies
questions.explanation: Derived lipids are substances produced from simple or compound lipids by hydrolysis. Examples include fatty acids, glycerol, steroids, fatty aldehydes, ketone bodies, fat-soluble vitamins, and carotenoids.

questions.questionText: Neutral lipids are called neutral because they are:
questions.explanation: Acylglycerols, cholesterol, and cholesterol esters are called neutral lipids because they do not carry an electrical charge.

questions.questionText: More than 90% of dietary lipids are usually:
questions.explanation: Most dietary fat is triacylglycerol. Smaller fractions include cholesterol, cholesterol esters, phospholipids, and free fatty acids.

questions.questionText: In adults, lipid digestion in the mouth and stomach is generally:
options: The major site of lipid digestion
questions.explanation: Dietary lipids are not digested to a major extent in the mouth or stomach in adults. Most lipid digestion occurs in the small intestine.

questions.questionText: Gastric lipase is more important in:
questions.explanation: Gastric lipase can degrade milk lipids and has a role in infants, but it is not a major lipid-digesting enzyme in adults.

questions.questionText: The main site of dietary lipid digestion is the:
questions.explanation: Most lipid digestion occurs in the small intestine, where bile salts and pancreatic enzymes act together.

questions.questionText: Emulsification of dietary lipids is important because it:
options: Increases surface area for enzymatic digestion
questions.explanation: Lipids are water-insoluble, and enzymes can act mainly at the surface of lipid droplets. Emulsification breaks large lipid droplets into smaller droplets, increasing the surface area.

questions.questionText: Emulsification is mainly achieved by:
options: Bile salts and mechanical mixing by peristalsis
questions.explanation: Bile salts act like detergents, and peristalsis mechanically mixes intestinal contents. Together, they emulsify dietary fat.

questions.questionText: Bile salts help lipid digestion because they have:
questions.explanation: Bile salts are amphipathic molecules. Their detergent action helps emulsify lipid droplets and makes lipid digestion more efficient.

questions.questionText: Cholecystokinin is secreted mainly in response to:
questions.explanation: Cholecystokinin, or CCK, is released from the lower duodenum in response to lipids. It stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion.

questions.questionText: CCK causes:
options: Gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme release
Complete closure of the intestinal lumen
questions.explanation: CCK promotes bile release from the gallbladder and digestive enzyme secretion from the pancreas, both important for lipid digestion.

questions.questionText: Secretin is released in response to:
options: High lipid storage in adipose tissue
questions.explanation: Secretin is released when acidic chyme enters the intestine. It stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion.

questions.questionText: Secretin stimulates secretion of:
questions.explanation: Bicarbonate neutralizes acidic chyme, creating a suitable pH for pancreatic digestive enzymes.

questions.questionText: Pancreatic lipase hydrolyzes triacylglycerol mainly at:
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase removes fatty acids from positions C1 and C3 of triacylglycerol, producing two free fatty acids and 2-monoacylglycerol.

questions.questionText: The main products of pancreatic lipase action on triacylglycerol are:
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase does not completely hydrolyze all ester bonds. It mainly produces free fatty acids from C1/C3 and leaves 2-monoacylglycerol.

questions.questionText: Colipase helps pancreatic lipase by:
questions.explanation: Colipase is secreted by the pancreas and helps pancreatic lipase remain active at the lipid-water interface.

questions.questionText: Cholesterol esterase hydrolyzes cholesterol esters to:
questions.explanation: Cholesterol esterase breaks cholesterol esters into free cholesterol and fatty acids.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A2 removes a fatty acid from which position of phospholipids?
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 removes the fatty acid at carbon 2 of a phospholipid, producing a lysophospholipid.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A2 is activated in the intestine by:
questions.explanation: The file states that phospholipase A2 is activated by trypsin in the intestine.

questions.questionText: Lecithin is another name for:
questions.explanation: Lecithin is phosphatidylcholine. When phospholipase A2 acts on it, lysolecithin is formed.

questions.questionText: The primary products of dietary lipid degradation absorbed by intestinal mucosal cells include:
options: Free fatty acids, free cholesterol, and 2-monoacylglycerol
questions.explanation: Dietary lipid digestion produces free fatty acids, free cholesterol, and 2-monoacylglycerol, which are absorbed by intestinal mucosal cells.

questions.questionText: Micelles are formed from lipid digestion products together with:
questions.explanation: Bile salts combine with lipid digestion products to form micelles. Micelles help transport lipids through the aqueous intestinal lumen to the brush border.

questions.questionText: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids differ from long-chain fatty acids because they:
options: Do not require micelle formation for absorption
Must be packaged into chylomicrons first
questions.explanation: Short- and medium-chain fatty acids are more water-soluble than long-chain fatty acids. They can be absorbed without needing micelle formation.
